one_line_takeaway: A tidy ANTLR-based C/C++ normalizer (SCoPE) that usefully de-duplicates and shrinks the flawed CVEFixes subset, but confirms that code minification does not help small code LLMs, which still detect vulnerabilities at roughly chance level (best F1 53%).
key_finding: SCoPE's normalization is effective as a data-cleaning tool (surfaced 905 duplicates, including label-inconsistent ones, and cut average function length from 374 to 300 tokens), but applying the size/vocabulary-reducing transformations before fine-tuning produced NO meaningful change in detection performance. All three models hovered near chance on the balanced task: NatGen best at 53% accuracy/F1 (before and after), CodeBERT 52% (F1 dropped 52->51 after), CodeGPT worst at 49% (unchanged). The authors conclude this corroborates prior claims that current LLMs are not yet ready for real-world software vulnerability detection, and that code minification does not rescue their performance.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SCoPE (Source Code Processing Engine), a configurable ANTLR4-based framework that reduces the size and normalizes C/C++ functions via five selectable transformations (renaming programmer-defined function/variable names, replacing strings with a generic token, whitespace normalization, comment removal, and tokenization). SCoPE is applied to clean the C/C++ subset of the widely used CVEFixes dataset: the authors' OWN result is that the normalization exposed 905 duplicate entries (including near-duplicates that differ only in comments or in renamed identifiers, some carrying contradictory vulnerable/non-vulnerable labels), yielding a refined, publicly released CVEFixes C/C++ subset. They then run a feature-representation study, fine-tuning three pre-trained code LLMs for binary software-vulnerability detection (SVD) on the code before vs. after applying SCoPE's minification. Note: the strong prior results (e.g., 65% accuracy AIBugHunter, >90% F1 VulnArmor, four-LLM 'not ready' study by Purba et al.) belong to CITED related work, not to this paper.
threat_model: This is a defensive software-security paper: the task is automated detection of vulnerabilities in real-world C/C++ source code (function-level binary classification of vulnerable vs. non-vulnerable), motivated by C/C++ prevalence in IoT devices and the cost of manual code review. The paper does NOT define an adversary or attack against the model itself (no evasion, poisoning, or prompt-injection threat model); its security framing is the detection task and, secondarily, data-quality flaws (mislabeled/duplicated entries) that can silently inflate reported SVD performance.
limitations: Authors' own threats to validity: (a) the erroneous-entry identification is automated, so undetected faulty data may remain; (b) the refined dataset is small, so conclusions may not hold on larger, more varied datasets. Additional reviewer-noted limits: ~23% of entries (3,614/15,649) were dropped solely because the ANTLR grammar cannot handle macros, biasing the retained set; balancing to 50/50 shrank the usable set to 9,224 entries; only three modestly sized (124M-220M) encoder/decoder code models are tested, no decoder-only instruction-tuned or larger LLMs; near-chance F1 (~49-53%) leaves little headroom to detect any transformation effect, so 'no significant impact' is weakly powered; and results are single-run without variance/statistical testing.

datasets_used: CVEFixes v1.0.7 C/C++ subset (real-world code, Zenodo record 7029359)
Extracted function-level subset: 15,649 entries (6,515 vulnerable, 9,134 non-vulnerable)
Balanced experimental dataset: 9,224 entries (50/50), split 80/10/10 stratified
CodeSearchNet (only as the pretraining corpus of the models used, not a dataset the authors trained on)
quantitative_results: 905 duplicate entries detected via SCoPE normalization
3,614 entries failed processing due to macros; 12,035 processed cleanly (of 15,649)
Average tokens per function reduced from 374 to 300 after SCoPE transformation
NatGen-B: 53% acc / 53% prec / 53% rec / 53% F1 (best model)
CodeGPT-B and CodeGPT-A: 49% across all metrics (worst model)
Abstract headline: best model achieves 53% F1-score
baselines_compared: CodeBERT (125M params, RoBERTa family, 512-token embeddings) fine-tuned before vs. after SCoPE
CodeGPT (124M params, GPT-2 family, 1024-token embeddings) before vs. after SCoPE
NatGen (220M params, Flan-T5 encoder-decoder, 512-token embeddings) before vs. after SCoPE
The comparison is internal (original code representation '-B' vs. SCoPE-processed '-A'); no external tool/model is re-run as a baseline in this paper
